segments.s351-g129: به + اسم introduces the indirect object: 'said to Gushtasp.' گفت is the verb 'said' placed after the indirect object (poetic word order). ای + نشسته دژم is a vocative: ای 'O' addresses Gushtasp and نشسته (past participle used adjectivally 'sitting/seated') plus the adjective دژم 'sullen/grim' form a descriptive phrase attached to the vocative.
segments.s351-g130: چه داری is a direct question with داری as 2nd-person present 'do you have / have you' (i.e. 'what do you have / what are you thinking'). ز here (= از) marks source or cause 'from/because of' and links اندیشه to the following noun. دل را uses را as the definite/affected-object marker ('the heart' as the thing affected by the thought). به غم indicates the resulting state 'into/with sorrow.' Note the compact, somewhat inverted poetic order where the object phrase دل را intervenes before the final locative/result phrase به غم.
segments.s351-g131: به روم 'to Rome' gives the destination. از بزرگان 'from among the great (men)' marks their class/origin. دو مهتر is the plural subject 'two chiefs/leaders.' بدند is an archaic poetic past/plural form (from بودن/شدن) meaning 'they were' or 'they came/appeared' — here the main past verb referring to those two chiefs.
segments.s351-g132: که introduces a relative clause qualifying the two chiefs: 'who ...'. The repeated با before each noun ('با تاج و با گنج و افسر') emphasizes accompaniment/possession — 'with a crown and with treasure and (with) a helmet.' The final بدند is the same plural past verb agreeing with the plural subject and here best read as 'they had/bore' or 'they came with.'
segments.s351-g133: یکی 'one' introduces an individual. آنک is a relative pronoun 'who/that' linking to the clause. نر اژدها را is the definite object 'the male dragon' with را marking it as definite/affected. بکشت is an archaic past form 'he killed' (old participial/past formation with b- prefix), the verb of the relative clause; subject is omitted and understood from context.
segments.s351-g134: فراوان بلا دید is a common phrase 'he experienced many calamities,' with دید the simple past 'saw/experienced.' و ننمود پشت links another past action: ننمود is the negated form of نمود 'showed' (ن- negative prefix) meaning 'did not show/display,' and پشت 'back' is the object — idiomatically 'did not show his back' = 'did not retreat/turn tail.' Both verbs are past and refer to the same subject introduced earlier.
segments.s351-g135: دگر آنک 'the other who...' parallels the previous 'one' construction. بر گرگ uses بر + noun to indicate the victim/target 'upon the wolf' (i.e. against the wolf). بدرید is the past tense 'tore' (archaic poetic past of دریدن), and پوست 'skin/hide' is the object; note the object appears without را — a common poetic omission.
segments.s351-g136: همه روم 'all (of) Rome' marks the extent/arena. یکسر is an adverb 'entirely/utterly' strengthening the predicate. پرآواز is an adjectival/epithetical word meaning 'renowned/celebrated' (literally 'full-voiced'), and اوست is the copula 'he is' (او + ست). The phrase therefore states that he was wholly famous throughout Rome.
segments.s351-g137: This is a prepositional phrase that sets the scene: به میدان قیصر = 'in/to the field/arena of Caesar.' The second به links the following nouns ننگ و نبرد ('shame and battle') as circumstances or qualities of that field; word order is topical (place first, then qualities).
segments.s351-g138: همی is a proclitic indicating continuous or iterative action ('they continuously/are raising'); به آسمان اندر = 'into/toward the sky' (two prepositions reinforcing direction); آرند is a classical 3rd‑person plural present/subjunctive of آوردن 'to bring/raise' (here 'they raise'); گرد here is the noun 'dust' that is being raised.
segments.s351-g139: نظاره (literally 'view' or 'gaze') plus the imperative شو ('become') is a classical idiom meaning 'look' or 'behold.' انجا 'there' specifies the place, and که introduces the relative clause قیصر بود 'where/when Caesar was' (بود is past 'was').
segments.s351-g140: مگر here functions as a rhetorical 'unless' or 'would not' introducing the counterfactual. بر دلت = 'upon your heart' (بر + دل with the 2nd‑person enclitic ت written separately), رنج کمتر بود = 'the pain would have been less' — بود in this construction conveys the past/conditional sense ('would have been').
segments.s351-g141: بدو گفت = 'he said to him' with the dative preposition بدو before the verb (inverted order). گشتاسپ is the speaker (proper name). کای is the archaic vocative particle 'O' and خوب‌چهر is the epithet 'fair‑faced' addressing the addressee.
segments.s351-g142: ز قیصر = 'from Caesar' or 'by Caesar.' مرا is the object 'me' split orthographically here into م + را; کی is the interrogative 'when/ever' (rhetorical), بود داد و مهر = 'did (he) give justice and favour' with داد 'give' and مهر 'affection/favour' — the clause expresses incredulity that Caesar ever bestowed such things on me.
segments.s351-g143: ترا (written here ت + را) is the object 'you,' با من 'with me,' از شهر بیرون 'out of the city,' and کند is the 3rd‑person singular present/subjunctive of کردن used here to indicate future/intent ('will drive out' / 'will do'). Word order places the objects and adverbials before the verb, as is common.
segments.s351-g144: چو = 'when'; بیند is the 3rd‑person form of دیدن 'to see' ('he/they see'); مرا (m + را) is 'me' as object; مردمی = 'the people' or 'the crowd'; چون here means 'how' or 'what (will he do)' and is paired with کند 'will do' to ask 'what will he do?' The clause is a rhetorical question contemplating the reaction of others.
segments.s351-g225: 'چو' introduces a temporal subordinate clause = 'when'. 'گشتاسپ' is the subject; 'برخاست' is the past tense verb 'rose/rose up'. 'از بامداد' is a prepositional/time phrase 'at/from dawn' specifying when the rising occurred.
segments.s351-g226: 'سر پرخرد' is a nominative noun phrase 'the wise‑headed (one)' with 'سر' (head) used metonymically for the person or his mind and 'پرخرد' as an adjectival modifier 'very wise'. 'سوی' is the preposition 'toward', 'قیصر' is the addressee 'the Caesar', and 'نهاد' is the past form of نهادن 'put/laid/turned' used idiomatically here as 'turned (his head) toward / approached'.
segments.s351-g227: Again 'چو' = 'when' introduces the clause. 'قیصر' is subject; 'ورا' is the object pronoun 'him' (referring to Gushtasp); 'دید' is the past tense 'saw'. 'خامش بماند' is a verbal phrase 'remained silent' (خامش = silent, بماند = remained), so 'when the Caesar saw him, he fell silent.'
segments.s351-g228: 'بران' functions as a demonstrative/adverbial 'there/that' pointing to the person; 'نامور' modifies him 'renowned'. 'پیشگاهش' means 'in his presence / at his court' (the possessive -ش refers to the Caesar), and 'نشاند' is the archaic past of نشاندن 'to seat' (here 'he seated him'). The clause says he caused the famed man to sit before him.
segments.s351-g229: This is a list of requested items with the verb 'خواست' 'asked/wanted' (past). 'کمر' here = 'girdle/belt' (object), 'از گنج' qualifies it as 'from the treasury', and 'و انگشتری' coordinates a second object 'and a ring'. The subject is omitted but understood from context ('he').
segments.s351-g230: 'یکی' introduces another single item 'one/a certain'; 'نامور' 'famed/renowned' modifies 'افسری' = 'diadem/ornamental headpiece'. 'مهتری' appositive/adjectival sense 'chief/preeminent', so the phrase lists 'one famed, chief diadem' as an additional requested gift.
segments.s351-g231: 'ببوسید' is past tense 'he kissed'; 'و پس' = 'and then', marking sequence. 'بر سر او نهاد' = 'placed upon his head' where 'بر' = on/upon, 'سر او' = his head, and 'نهاد' (past of نهادن) = 'put/placed' — the direct object (the diadem) is omitted but implied.
segments.s351-g232: 'ز' (poetic for 'از') = 'from/of'; 'کار گذشته' = 'past deeds'. 'بسی' here functions adverbially 'much/many', and 'کرد یاد' is the verbal idiom 'to make remembrance' = 'remembered'. Literally 'from past deeds he made much remembrance' = 'he recalled many past deeds.'
segments.s351-g17: سبک (adjective) precedes خنجر (noun) in the normal Persian adjective–noun order; here سبک means “light (in weight)”. اندر is the archaic preposition “in/into” placed before the possessed noun دهانش; دهانش = دهان + enclitic -ش (“its/his”). نهاد is the 3rd-person singular past of نهادن “to put/place.” The whole segment reads literally “(he) placed the light dagger in its mouth.”
segments.s351-g18: ز is the poetic preposition “from/of”; دادار is the Creator (subject of the genitive notion); نیکی دهش is a nominal phrase “goodness/gift (that was) given” where دهش is a noun (“a giving/gift”). کرد یاد is an idiomatic compound where کرد (past of کردن) + یاد (remembrance) = “he remembered.” The segment thus says he remembered the goodness/gift from the Creator.
segments.s351-g19: بزد is 3rd-person singular past “he struck/smote.” تیز دندان is an adjective + noun compound “sharp-tooth / sharp fang.” بدان is an archaic instrumental expression equivalent to Modern Persian با آن “with that” (here written as the elements ب + دان in the word list); خنجرش = خنجر + enclitic -ش “his/its dagger.” So the clause means “he struck the sharp tooth with that/his dagger.” Note: 'دان' in the list is not lexicalized here as ‘to know’ but part of the archaic compound ب + آن.
segments.s351-g20: همه تیغها is the subject “all the blades/swords.” شد is the past copular/verb “became/were”; به کام is a prepositional phrase meaning “to the satisfaction/into the mouth” (literally “to desire/fulfillment”); اندرش = اندر + -ش “in/within it.” The clause literally means “all the blades became to/in its satisfaction,” i.e. they ended up in its mouth.
segments.s351-g21: به before nouns here marks instrument/means (“with/ by”): به زهر و به خون = “with poison and with blood.” کوه is the object “the hill/mass/mountain.” یکسر is an adverb “entirely/altogether,” and بشست is 3rd-person past “he washed/bathed.” Word order places the affected noun(s) before the verb, a common poetic order.
segments.s351-g22: همی is a durative/continuous adverb (here “continually/kept on”); ریخت in this context is the intransitive/passage sense “poured/flowed” (appearance of liquid). زو = ز + او/او elided = “from it.” تا + گشت introduces a result clause “until it became,” where گشت is past of گشتن “became/turned” and سست is the adjective “weak.”
segments.s351-g23: به شمشیر here indicates the instrument/way (“by/with the sword”); آن زمان = “at that time.” دست شیر is the subject phrase “the lion’s hand/paw.” برد is used as a past action meaning “took/seized” (archaic/poetic past of بردن 'to take up'); the poetic order places the verb before the subject phrase. (The supplied sense list for برد did not include the 'took' sense, so that lemma is marked -1 in senses.)
segments.s351-g24: بزد is 3rd-person past “he struck.” بر سر is the prepositional phrase “upon/on the head of.” اژدهای دلیر is a noun phrase with genitive/epithet order “the brave dragon.” Together: “(he) struck upon the head of the brave dragon.”
segments.s351-g25: همی is an older/perfective-continuous particle here marking ongoing or habitual action in narrative past (literary 'was/kept'). ریخت is the past (perfect) of ریختن "to pour/spill," so together همی ریخت = "(it) kept pouring/was poured." مغزش is مغز + the enclitic possessive -ش "his/its brain." بران is a fused prepositional phrase (بر + آن) meaning "upon that/onto it." سنگ سخت is a simple noun phrase with adjective after noun (stone + hard). The whole segment describes the brain repeatedly spilling onto that hard stone.
segments.s351-g26: ز is the poetic preposition "from/out of." باره here means the occasion/strike (an instance, i.e. the blow). درآمد is used in the sense of "came forth/occurred" (literary past). گو is the quotative/introductory particle meaning "he said" (introducing direct speech), and نیکبخت is a vocative/exclamation "fortunate one" or "O lucky one." Grammatically, this short clause links the physical action to the utterance: "from that blow he uttered 'O fortunate one.'"
segments.s351-g27: بکند is an archaic/past form (prefix ب‑ plus the root کندن) meaning "he tore/pulled out" in narrative past — in epic Persian the b‑ prefix often appears on past verbs. از + دهانش = "from its/his mouth" (دهان + possessive -ش). دو دندان نخست is a numeral + noun + adjective order: "the two front/first teeth." The clause describes a completed action: he pulled two front teeth out of the mouth.
segments.s351-g28: پس آنگه are sequential adverbs meaning "then/after that." بیامد is past "came" (he came forward/approached). سر و تن is a coordinated noun phrase "head and body." بشست is the past of شستن "he washed." Together the clause states the subsequent actions: then he came and washed head and body (the objects of washing are placed after the verb as in classical Persian narrative).
segments.s351-g29: خروشان is an adverbial/participle meaning "roaring/tumultuously." بغلتید is a past plural verb meaning "they rolled/tumbled" (or "they embraced/rolled over" depending on sense); here it describes bodies or living things rolling. بر خاک بر uses repeated بر for emphasis or poetic redundancy; بر خاک = "upon the earth/dust." The clause paints the motion (they rolled onto the ground with a roar).
segments.s351-g30: به پیش is a prepositional phrase meaning "in the presence of / before." خداوند is a title that can mean "God" or "Lord; sovereign" and here functions as the addressee/figure before whom they fall. پیروزگر is an attributive adjective/noun meaning "victorious/triumphant," qualifying خداوند. The whole phrase indicates direction/location: "before the triumphant Lord."
segments.s351-g31: کجا is the interrogative "where." داد is the past of دادن "gave" but in rhetorical or idiomatic contexts can imply "stood"/"was (provided)" or more loosely "had effect"; here it is part of a rhetorical question about the presence or efficacy of something. آن دستگاه بزرگ is a noun phrase (آن = "that," دستگاه = "establishment/court/power," بزرگ = "great"). Syntactically this asks about the whereabouts or efficacy of that great power.
segments.s351-g32: بران is (as above) a fused prepositional unit (بر + آن) meaning "against/on that" and heads the object of the question. گرگ و آن اژدهای سترگ names the opponents: گرگ = "that wolf," و = "and," آن اژدهای = "that dragon of..." سترگ is an epithet meaning large/mighty here; the whole segment specifies "against that wolf and that mighty dragon." Grammatically it completes the rhetorical question about where the great power was when faced with these foes.
segments.s351-g121: 'به میدان' is a prepositional phrase 'into/onto the arena' (به = toward/into + میدان = arena). 'شدندی' is an archaic past plural (شد- + -ندی) meaning 'they came/appeared' or 'they entered'; the following noun phrase 'دو داماد اوی' is the subject ('his two grooms') with اوی as a possessive 'his'. Note word order: classical Persian often places the locative phrase before the verb and leaves the possessor-marked noun after the verb.
segments.s351-g122: 'بیاراستندی' is an archaic past plural verbal form (بی‌آراستن → بیاراستند) meaning 'they adorned/made (something)'; here it functions with a direct-object phrase. 'دل شاد اوی' = 'his heart glad' is the object phrase ('دل' = heart; 'شاد' = glad; 'اوی' = his). In older Persian the verb can precede the object phrase as here, and the suffix -ندی marks plural past.
segments.s351-g123: This is a list of instrumental phrases introduced by repeated 'به' used in the sense 'by/with' (instruments or means): 'به تیر' (by/with arrow), 'به چوگان' (by/with the polo‑mallet), 'و زخم سنان' (and spear‑wounds). The conjunction و links the items; each 'به' marks the instrument.
segments.s351-g124: 'بهر دانشی' is an adverbial phrase 'for the sake of skill/learning' (بهر = for/toward; دانشی = 'knowledge/skill'). 'گرد کرده عنان' is a light verbal phrase: گرد (around/about) + کرده (past participle of کردن) + عنان (reins) — literally 'having turned the reins around' = 'they turned/wheeled their reins' (i.e. they guided their horses about). Here کرده is the participial auxiliary forming the perfective expression.
segments.s351-g125: همی is a frequent archaic/poetic adverb here meaning 'still/ongoing' or 'kept on'. تاختندی is a past (poetic) 3rd-person plural form meaning 'they rode/galloped/charged' (from تاختن). چپ و دست راست literally lists directions/parts 'left and (the) right hand' — idiomatically 'to the left and to the right' or 'on both flanks'. Word order places the adverb before the past verb, a typical older style: 'They were still charging left and right.'
segments.s351-g126: که here introduces a subordinate clause giving the reason or echoing a prior remark ('as/that'). گفتی in context functions to mean 'as (you) said' or 'as it was said' (the text uses a conversational/quotative sense). سواری is 'mounted troops / horsemen' (a collective meaning), بدیشان is the dative 'to them/for them', and سزاست is a present/factual predicate 'it is fitting/deserved'. Together: 'for — as you said — mounted riders suit them.'
segments.s351-g127: چنین is an adverb meaning “thus/so.” تا here is a conjunction meaning “when/while” (classical usage: 'چنین تا' = 'so that when' or 'thus, when'). برآمد is a simple past (3rd person singular) meaning “arose/appeared.” برین روزگار is a noun phrase: برین is an adjectival formation (بر + -ین) meaning “exalted/foremost” modifying روزگار “time/day”; together it yields “that exalted time/day.” The whole segment functions as a temporal clause 'Thus, when that exalted day arose...' connecting to the main clause.
segments.s351-g128: بیامد is a past-tense verb (3rd person singular) meaning “came/arrived”; in epic Persian the verb often precedes its subject. کتایون is a proper name (Katayun). آموزگار is an appositive noun following the name meaning “teacher/mentor,” so the sequence literally reads 'came Katayun, (the) teacher' = 'Katayun, the teacher, arrived.'
segments.s351-g97: This is a short past-tense clause in which 'بران' (thereupon) adverbially sets the time/sequence, 'اژدها' is the subject, 'بر یکی' is a prepositional phrase meaning "for one / for someone" (object of the celebration), and 'جشن کرد' is the simple past tense 'held a feast'. Word order is typical Persian: adverb – subject – prepositional object – verb.
segments.s351-g98: This is a temporal/result clause introduced with the preposition 'ز' (here = "from / at" in poetic usage). 'شبگیر' functions as a noun-like time expression ("nightfall" / "night-visitor"), 'تا' is the temporal conjunction 'until', and 'شد' is the past of 'to become' governing the nominative 'جهان' (the world) which takes the predicate 'لاژورد' (lapis-blue). The clause means that with night the world became blue.
segments.s351-g99: 'چو' here = 'when'; it introduces a temporal subordinate clause. 'خورشید' is the subject and 'بنهاد' (past of 'نهادن') means 'placed' or 'set'; the verb takes the prepositional phrase 'بر چرخ' ('upon the sky/wheel') and the direct object 'تاج' (crown). Word order is subject–verb–object with the prepositional phrase marking location.
segments.s351-g100: 'به کردار' is an instrumental/comparative phrase 'in the manner/likeness of'; here it qualifes the result on the following predicate. 'زر' is the simile term 'gold', 'آب شد' is a poetic compound meaning 'became like water / melted' (here 'took on the molten/flowing hue'), and 'روی عاج' ('face of ivory') is the thing affected. The main verb 'شد' is past 'became'.
segments.s351-g101: 'فرستاده قیصر' is a nominative noun phrase 'Caesar's envoy'. 'سقف را' is the definite object (marked by 'را'); 'بخواند' is an older/narrative past here meaning 'he summoned/called (to read)' or 'had (it) read' — the b- prefix occurs in classical style but the whole forms a past action by the envoy.
segments.s351-g102: This coordinate clause lists subsequent actions. 'بپرسید' is the past 'he asked'; 'و' = 'and'; 'بر تخت زرین' is a locative/goal phrase 'onto the golden throne'; 'نشاند' is an archaic past of 'نشاندن' meaning 'set/placed/caused to sit' (here 'seated him'). The verb governs the preceding prepositional phrase as the place where the person was seated.
segments.s351-g103: 'ز' is the poetic preposition 'from'; 'بطریق' ('به طریق') means 'by the way of / through / from the road'; the orthographic cluster 'وز' here is best read as an archaic contraction 'و ز' = 'and from' (a conjunction + preposition), and 'جاثلیقان شهر' means 'the Catholicoi of the city'. The whole string lists sources/groups coming from those places.
segments.s351-g104: 'هرانکس' = 'whoever/anyone'; 'کش' here functions as an agentive/relational element (archaic use, see note) attached to describe a kind of person; 'از' = 'from / among', 'مردمی' = 'the people' (adjectival/noun), 'بود' is past 'was', and 'بهر' means 'for / to (serve a purpose)'. Together this yields a generic clause 'whoever among the people was (so) / was of use'.
segments.s351-g89: همی آمد is the continuous/imperfective past marker + verb (literally “kept coming”); از چرخ is a prepositional phrase “from the sky/heavens”; بانگ چکاو is a genitive phrase “the cry (banġ) of the chakaav (lark)”. Word order is subject/agentless verb phrase + source + the sound’s genitive modifier (typical classical Persian compactness).
segments.s351-g90: This clause reports speech. تو گفتی literally reads “you said” (تو = 2nd-person pronoun, گفتی = perfect/past of گفتن), but in classical narrative a spoken utterance is often embedded without quotation punctuation; دارد/ندارد is the present-tense possessive/existential auxiliary (negated here: ندارد = “does not have”). تن گاو is the noun phrase “the body of the cow” (تن = body; گاو = cow) and تاو is the predicate noun “radiance/glow” coming after ندارد. The whole yields direct speech content: “(it/you) said: the cow’s body has no radiance.”
segments.s351-g91: هرانکس که is a free relative “whoever/whosoever” + complementizer که; آن زخم شمشیر is a noun phrase with demonstrative + head + modifier “that wound of the sword”; دید is simple past third-person singular “saw/observed.” The clause is a protasis introducing the result clause that follows.
segments.s351-g92: خروشیدن is the verbal noun/infinitive “the roaring” or “to roar” used as the content of what was heard; گاو گردون is a compound “cow of the sky/heaven” (گردون = sky/fate modifying گاو); شنید is past tense “heard.” The structure is object (verbal noun) + possessor phrase + verb: “heard the roar of the sky-cow.”
segments.s351-g93: همی گفت is the iterative past (“kept saying”); کاین is a contraction of که + این “that this”; خنجر is the subject/predicand “dagger”; اهرنست here is a predicative nominal (archaic/specialized form) functioning like “is Ahrn/Ernest” — i.e., a nominal identification. Classical Persian often fuses the copula into the predicate noun in such set phrases.
segments.s351-g94: وگر is a coordinating/concessive particle often glossed “and/and that” or “but if” in classical usage; here it links the alternative report. زخم شیراوژن is the genitive noun phrase “the wound of Shirāvajān” (proper name + noun); آهرمنست is the predicate meaning “is Ahriman’s” where آهرمن (Ahriman) + ـست (is) form the identification. The clause thus reports the other view: that the wound belongs to Ahriman.
segments.s351-g95: همانگاه is an adverb meaning “immediately/at that moment”; قیصر is the subject “the Caesar/emperor”; ز ایوان is a prepositional phrase “from the iwan/portico” (ز = poetic from); براند is past-tense “drove away/expelled.” The verb applies to an understood object (those who had spoken or caused the disturbance).
segments.s351-g96: بزرگان و فرزانگان را is the direct object marked with را (definite/object marker) meaning “the great men and the sages”; بخواند is past-tense of خواندن used here in the sense “to call/summon” (classical usage often uses خواندن = to summon). Together: “he summoned the great and the wise.”
segments.s351-g201: ز here is the poetic preposition 'from/of' placed before the names; the sequence 'ز میرین و اهرن برآشفت و گفت' has two past verbs: برآشفت (past) 'became angry/rose in anger' and گفت (past) 'said', with و linking clauses. The verb گفت introduces a reported clause (handled by the following که).
segments.s351-g202: که is the quotative/complementizer 'that' introducing reported speech. نماند is past tense 'remained/was left' used here with هرگز to give the negative sense 'never remained'; سخن is the object 'word/speech' and در نهفت (literally 'in hidden') means 'hidden/kept concealed'. Word order places the verb (نماند) before the noun (سخن) in this classical phrasing.
segments.s351-g203: همانگه is an adverb 'at that moment/straightaway'. نشست is past 'sat/down' used here of alighting (classical Persian uses نشست 'sat' or 'settled' for dismounting). The prepositional phrase از بر بادپای means 'from off the back of the swift-footed (mount)'—از (from) + بر (off/upon) + بادپای (the swift-footed one, a name or epithet of a mount).
segments.s351-g204: به + noun + بیامد is a classical collocation meaning 'he came with/into (doing) X' — here به پوزش بیامد = 'he came offering an apology' (بیامد is past 'came'). بر پاک رای links his action to his intention: بر here functions roughly 'for/toward' and پاک رای ('pure counsel/intent') describes the motive he wishes to present or vindicate.
segments.s351-g205: بسی is an intensifier 'very/much'; آفرین کرد is a periphrastic expression 'made praise' = 'praised' (کرد is the past auxiliary/verb 'did/made'). فرزند را is the definite object marked by را, so the whole phrase means 'he praised the son greatly.'
segments.s351-g206: This segment is an appositive identifying the praised child: مران is a proper name; پاک‌دامن and خردمند are attributive epithets ('chaste/pure-garmented' and 'wise') describing him. The object marker را attaches at the end of the phrase, marking the whole phrase as the object in apposition to فرزند را.
segments.s351-g207: بدو here is the dative/adverbial 'to him' (he said to him). گفت is past 'said' and قیصر is the speaker 'the Caesar'. The sequence that follows contains a vocative: که‌ای ماهروی should be read as the quotative/that + vocative 'ای ماهروی' — 'that (he said): "O moon-faced one..."' The vocative 'ای ماهروی' addresses the addressee directly.
segments.s351-g208: گزیدی is past 'you chose' (2nd person past). تو is the pronoun 'you' (here emphatic). اندر is the classical preposition 'into/within'. خور خویش is a possessive phrase where خوی + ش is 'one's own' and خور functions as 'that which consumes/food/prey' (here idiomatically 'undoing' or 'that which devours you'); شوی (become) is final and governs the clause: 'you have chosen to become (subject to) your own eater/undoing.' Note that ش is the enclitic possessive 'his/her/own' attached to خوی.
segments.s351-g177: چه is the interrogative 'what' modifying the following noun phrase. نامی here functions as 'name' (classical usage) and بمن گوی is an imperative-like directive 'say to me' (گوی used in classical/poetic register for 'say'). شهر و نژاد are coordinate complements ('city and lineage') listing the items asked for; the sequence is noun phrase + indirect object + verb (typical older Persian word order).
segments.s351-g178: ورا is the indirect object 'to him' (referring back to some person). زین سخن here is a prepositional/phrasing meaning 'to/with regard to this speech/question' (classical z‑in). هیچ is the negative quantifier 'no/none' modifying پاسخ 'answer', and نداد is the simple past 'gave' (داد) used negatively with 'هیچ' to mean 'gave no reply'.
segments.s351-g179: چنین گفت = 'thus said' (چنین adverb 'thus/so' + گفت 'said' — classical past of گفتن). کان is a demonstrative/classical particle 'that' (here introducing the appositive noun phrase). خوار بیگانه مرد is an attributive noun phrase 'a lowly/ despised foreign man' in which adjectives precede the head 'مرد' (poetic diction).
segments.s351-g180: که introduces a relative clause 'who/that'. از شهرقیصر marks origin 'from the Caesar's city' (از = 'from', شهرقیصر = 'the emperor's/imperial city'). ورا is the object 'him', and دور کرد is a verb phrase 'made (him) far' i.e. 'drove him away' (دور 'far' + کرد 'did/made' yields 'drove away'). Word order here is agent/source (از...) + object + verb, typical of classical Persian.
segments.s351-g181: چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when'. داماد گشتم is 'I became (a) son‑in‑law' (گشتم = 'I became' from گشتن used intransitively). ز شهرم indicates source 'from my city' and براند is a past transitive verb 'drove away' or 'was driven away' (in context it reads as 'I was driven out' though morphologically 3rd-person form can indicate general agent).
segments.s351-g182: کس ... نخواند here functions as a negative indefinite construction 'no one ... read/called'. کس in this negative context means 'no one'. از دفترش literally 'from his office/registry' (دفتر used as 'office/record'), نام من 'my name' (possessive expressed postpositively), and بر نخواند (بر + خواند) means 'read aloud/recited' — نخواند is past negative 'did not read'.
segments.s351-g183: ز قیصر ستم = 'oppression from Caesar' (ز = 'from', قیصر = 'Caesar/emperor', ستم = 'oppression'). بر کتایون رسید uses رسید (simple past 'reached'/'befell') with بر marking the target 'upon Katayun' — literal 'oppression from Caesar reached upon Katayun' = 'Katayun suffered oppression from the emperor.'
segments.s351-g184: که introduces a clause describing Katayun's response: مردی غریب 'a strange/foreign man' is the direct object, and از میان برگزید 'chose from among [them]' uses the partitive/particularizing از میان = 'from among' and برگزید (past) 'chose/selected'. The clause is relative/explanatory to the previous statement.
segments.s351-g81: خود is an emphatic reflexive pronoun 'himself' placed before the verb to stress the subject. از پیش is a prepositional phrase 'ahead of / before' (از = from, پیش = before/in front of) and takes the following nouns گاوان و گردون as its complement ('the herds and the sky/heaven'). برفت is an archaic past-tense verb meaning 'he went away/advanced' (past 3rd-person), so the whole clause reads literally 'he himself went ahead of the herds and the heaven.'
segments.s351-g82: به نزدیک قیصر is a locative phrase formed with به + نزدیک (to/toward 'near Caesar'), functioning as an adverbial of place. خرامید is a past‑tense, 3rd‑person singular verb 'he strode/strutted/pranced' describing manner of approach. تفت appears here as a short interjection/onomatopoetic particle or an isolated word not matching the listed lexical senses (it functions like an emphatic sound or exclamation following the verb).
segments.s351-g83: به روم اندرون is a place expression (به + place + اندرون) meaning 'into/at Rome's inner quarters' or 'within Rome.' آگهی is 'news/information' and یافتند is past tense, 3rd‑person plural 'they obtained/found' — together 'they received/learned the news inside Rome.' The verb is plural to agree with an understood plural subject (made explicit in the next segment).
segments.s351-g84: جهاندیدگان is the nominative plural 'the world‑seen' — an epithet for experienced, worldly-wise men or veterans. پیش here functions adverbially as 'forward / to the front / before (someone)' and بشتافتند is past plural 'they hurried' or 'rushed.' The segment thus says 'the experienced men rushed forward.'
segments.s351-g85: چو is a conjunction 'when.' گاو is the subject 'the ox/cow.' اندر آمد is a two-part verbal phrase (اندر + آمد) meaning 'entered / came in' (past). به هامون ز کوه gives direction and source: به هامون = 'into/to the plain' (به + place) and ز کوه = 'from the mountain' (ز = from, followed by the source noun). Word order is typical classical Persian: temporal clause (چو ...) + subject + verb + place/source.
segments.s351-g86: خروشی بد is a noun plus qualifier meaning 'a dreadful/terrible roar' (خروشی = roar/commotion, بد = 'bad/terrible' used as intensifier). اندر میان گروه is a prepositional phrase 'in the middle of the host/among the company' (اندر میان = 'within the midst of', گروه = troop/assembly). Together: 'a terrible roar (arose) in the midst of the host.'
segments.s351-g87: ازان is an archaic contracted form of از آن 'from that' and introduces causes. زخم 'wound' and آن اژدهای دژم 'that grim dragon' are conjoined causes (و = and); دژم is an adjective 'grim / sullen' qualifying the dragon. The phrase lists the two harmful factors 'from that wound and that grim dragon.'
segments.s351-g88: کزان is a relative/prepositional compound 'from which' referring back to the previous causes. بود is the past copula 'was' (3rd‑person singular). بر indicates the target 'upon' (بر گاو گردون = 'upon the celestial ox' — گردون here = the vault of heaven/the celestial sphere used as epithet with گاو), and ستم means 'oppression / harm.' Grammatically this is a relative clause: 'from which was upon the celestial ox oppression.'
segments.s351-g113: This is a subordinate clause introduced by که (that). کس here with the negative verb that follows functions as an indefinite used in a negative sense ('no one'). چون + دو داماد من is a simile/comparison phrase 'like two grooms of mine' (چون = 'like/as'), and در جهان ('in the world') gives the locus. Word order is typical Persian: Topic (کس چون دو داماد من) + locative (در جهان).
segments.s351-g114: نبینند is 3rd-person plural negative present/subjunctive used here to mean 'do not / have not seen' (classical Persian often uses this form for general negation). بیش از is a comparative 'more than' (بیش = more; از = than/of). کهان و مهان are the two items being compared to (a poetic pair). The clause as a whole means 'they do not see (i.e. no one has seen) [anyone] more than ...'.
segments.s351-g115: نوشتند is past tense, 3rd-person plural 'they wrote'. نامه is the direct object 'letters'. به هر مهتری is a dative phrase 'to every مهتری' (هر = every; مهتری here means 'chief/leader, elder'). The order is Verb + Object + Indirect object, a normal Persian word order in narrative past tense.
segments.s351-g116: This is the content of the letters posed as a question. کجا is 'where'. داشتی is the 2nd-person singular past/imperfect of داشتن ('you had' / 'did you have'); in reported/question form it asks 'where did you have/obtain'. تخت‌گر افسری is a compound: تخت‌گر 'throne‑maker' (one who makes or provides a throne) and افسری 'diadem/helmet/diadem-like ornament' — literal rendering: 'a throne‑maker and a diadem'.
segments.s351-g117: که introduces a clause giving the result or circumstance. نر اژدها is a determiner + noun 'the male/masculine dragon' (نر = male). با here links the next noun and functions like 'and/with' to join 'سرافراز گرگ' ('the proud/lofty wolf') — together: 'the male dragon and the lofty wolf'. The adjective سرافراز precedes the noun it modifies, as is common in Persian poetic diction.
segments.s351-g118: تبه شد is a compound of an adjective/verb stem 'تبه' (ruin, mischief, cf. 'be ruined') + شد 'became/was' — here passive/resultative 'were ruined' or 'came to ruin'. به دست is the agent/instrumental construction 'by the hand(s) of'. دو مرد سترگ is the agent: 'two men ...' — in context 'mighty/heroic men'; note that سترگ is an archaic/poetic adjective meaning 'mighty/large' (not to be confused with the literal senses in the given list).
segments.s351-g119: یکی here means 'one' or 'a certain' (introducing a single instance). منظری is a noun 'a spectacle/scene/display'. پیش ایوان خویش is a locative phrase 'before/in front of his iwan' (پیش = before; ایوان = iwan/porch). خویش is expressed as the two-part sequence خوی + ش (kin/own + possessive suffix) so together mean 'his own'.
segments.s351-g120: برآورده is a past-participial/perfect form meaning 'raised/erected/placed'. چون introduces a simile 'like/as'. تخت رخشان خویش literally 'his shining throne' (تخت = throne; رخشان = radiant/illustrious; again خویش = 'his own' split into خوی + ش). The whole segment states that the spectacle was put up, 'like his radiant throne.'
segments.s351-g105: به پیش + NAME is a prepositional phrase meaning 'into the presence of' or 'before (someone)'; here به (‘to/into’) comes before پیش (‘presence/of’) and the proper name سکوبا follows. شدند is the past plural of شدن used as the main verb 'they went/were brought' (literally 'they became' in older usage), so the phrase means 'they went/approached Skuba.' Word order is preposition + locative + verb, typical of Persian narrative.
segments.s351-g106: This is a short noun phrase qualifying the assembly: جهاندیده ('worldly‑wise') modifies انجمن implicitly (the adjective may stand before or after the noun in poetry). با + قیصر 'with the Caesar' links that group to the emperor; و رای زن 'and (with) the counsel/opinion of the woman' attaches as an additional accompaniment or influencing factor. با is the standard 'with', رای here means 'counsel/plan', and زن is 'the woman' (likely the queen). The whole segment enumerates those present/in agreement.
segments.s351-g107: به + proper name (اهرن) marks the indirect object/recipient 'to Ahran'. سپردند is past‑tense, 3rd‑person plural 'they entrusted/handed (over)'. پس 'then/after' sequences the action; دخترش 'his daughter' is the thing entrusted (object), with the possessive -ش attached to دختر. Word order is verb-final in poetry but the sequence here is prepositional phrase + verb + adverb + object.
segments.s351-g108: به دستوری مهربان مادرش is an instrumental/agentive phrase: به + دستوری 'by/under the command/instruction' (here 'به' functions like English 'under/by') and مهربان modifies دستور (a kind/kindly instruction). مادرش 'her mother' (mother + attached possessive) indicates whose instruction it was. Grammatically this gives the manner/guardian role in which the daughter was left.
segments.s351-g109: ز ایوان 'from the iwan/porch' uses the poetic preposition ز (from). چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing the subordinate clause; مردم پراکنده شد is a simple past clause 'the people were scattered/dispersed' (پراکنده شد = 'became scattered'). The verb شد is past and agrees with مردم (plural) in meaning despite the poetic compactness.
segments.s351-g110: دل نامور 'the renowned/celebrated heart' is the subject; زان سخن 'by/through that speech' uses زان ('from/through that') to indicate cause or instrument. زنده شد is a common poetic collocation meaning 'was made alive' or 'was revived' (زنده = alive; شد = became). Together: 'the renowned heart was revived by that speech.'
segments.s351-g111: چنین گفت is the usual formula 'thus said' (چنین = 'thus/so', گفت = 'said'); کامروز 'this very day/today' is an emphatic temporal adverb, followed by روز منست 'is my day' — منست here is an older contracted copula construction meaning 'is mine / belongs to me' or more loosely 'is fitting to me.' The clause functions as direct speech introduced by the reporting verb.
segments.s351-g112: This is a parallel predicate in the reported speech: بلند آسمان 'the lofty/heaven-high sky' (an emphatic epithet) followed by دلفروز (a compound adjective 'heart‑brightening, heart‑cheering') and the predicate منست (same contracted copula as above). The two nominal phrases are copular complements asserting possession or destiny — poetic, elevated diction common in Ferdowsi.
segments.s351-g49: به اهرن is a prepositional phrase meaning “to Ahrun” (به = to + proper name). چنين گفت is a verb phrase “(he) said thus/so”: چنين (thus) precedes the past tense verb گفت (said). کان is an archaic/poetic particle introducing a reported clause meaning roughly “that” (classical usage). Word order is typical old/poetic Persian: indirect object + discourse marker + verb + clause-introducer.
segments.s351-g50: بدین is the prepositional phrase 'by/with this' (classical written as ب + این; here segmented as ب + دین). خنجر is the direct object 'dagger'. تیز شد is a two-word predication where تیز (sharp) is the adjective and شد (past of شدن) is the light verb meaning 'became / was made' — so 'was made sharp.' بی‌بها is an adjectival complement meaning 'without price / priceless' describing the result.
segments.s351-g51: شما is the 2nd-person plural/formal pronoun 'you'. از دم اژدهای بزرگ is a prepositional phrase 'from/by the breath of the great dragon' where از marks source/agent and دم (breath) is the noun modified by the genitive اژدهای (dragon) and the adjective بزرگ (great) follows the noun in typical Persian order.
segments.s351-g52: پر از بیم گشتید is the core verbal phrase: پر از = 'full of', بیم = 'fear', and گشتید is the past-tense 2nd-person-plural of گشتن used here as 'became' (you became filled). The following از کار گرگ gives the reason/causal phrase 'because of / on account of the wolf’s deed' (از + noun phrase), with کار گرگ = 'the wolf’s deed/act.'
segments.s351-g53: مرا here is the clitic/object-like marker meaning 'for me' or 'mine' (word is split in the data; functionally 'مرا' = 'for me' / topicalizer). کارزار دلاور سران is a nominal string: کارزار = 'battle/field', دلاور = 'valiant/brave' (adjective or attributive noun) and سران = 'chiefs/leaders' (plural). The phrase thus states possession/topic: 'as for me: the battle of brave leaders...'.
segments.s351-g54: سرافراز is an adjectival epithet 'proud / exalted' modifying the leaders; با introduces an instrumental/comitative phrase 'with'. گرزهای is the plural possessive/construct of گرز ('maces / clubs') and گران modifies it: in this context گران is adjectival 'heavy' (or 'weighty/valuable'); together 'with heavy maces'. The whole segment continues the nominal description of the battle/participants.
segments.s351-g55: بسی تیز آید ز جنگ نهنگ is a clause where بسی is an intensifier 'very' (classical adverb), تیز 'sharp/keen' is the quality, and آید is the present/future-like third-person singular of آمدن 'comes' used here to describe an action/result. ز جنگ نهنگ is an ablative phrase 'from the whale's battle/struggle' (ز = from, جنگ = fight, نهنگ = whale/giant).
segments.s351-g56: که introduces a relative clause 'which / that' modifying the coming strike. از ژرف برآید به جنگ contains از ژرف 'from the deep' (ژرف = deep, از = from), برآید (برآمدن) 'rises/appears' in present/subjunctive sense, and به جنگ 'to/into battle' (به + جنگ). Together: 'which rises from the depths to the battle.'
segments.s351-g33: همی is a durative/adverbial particle meaning ‘kept/continually’ and precedes the past tense verb گفت, producing a continuous past sense (‘kept saying’). The speakers (لهراسپ و فرخ زریر) follow the verb in the poetic verb–subject order; و simply links the two names.
segments.s351-g34: شدند is the past plural of شدن ‘become’ (they became) and refers back to the previously named speakers. از + تن و جان is an ablative/extent phrase ‘in/with respect to body and soul,’ and سیر is used predicatively (‘sated, full’); گشتاسپ is the patient/theme named (Gushtasp) placed after the predicate in poetic word order.
segments.s351-g35: به introduces a clustered list of qualities/instruments (‘with’ or ‘in’), here attaching to the series روشن‌روان و دل و زور و تاب (a compound of poetic nouns meaning ‘clear/bright soul, heart, strength, and radiance/vigor’). The nouns are coordinated by و; روشن‌روان is a compounded epithet typical of epic poetry.
segments.s351-g36: همانا is an emphatic adverb ‘truly/indeed.’ نبینند is third-person plural negative of دیدن (‘they do not see’), here stating a general/epic truth. ما را is the object ‘us’ marked by را, and به خواب is a prepositional phrase ‘in sleep’ or ‘in dreams.’
segments.s351-g37: بجز is a preposition ‘except.’ The coordinated nouns رنج و سختی (‘suffering and hardship’) are the excluded content; نبینم is first-person singular negative present/subjunctive (‘I see not’ / ‘I do not behold’). ز (poetic form of از) + دهر means ‘from/of the world/time/age’ — a common epic phrase.
segments.s351-g38: پراگنده is an adjective ‘scattered/widespread’ and here describes the following noun phrase. بر جای is ‘upon the place/land,’ تریاک is literally ‘opium’ used figuratively, and زهر is ‘poison’; together they form a terse poetic image: a poison like opium spread over the land.
segments.s351-g39: مگر here introduces an exception/conditional meaning ‘unless’ (i.e., ‘if not’). زندگانی ‘life’ is the object of the verb دهد ‘gives’ (present/subjunctive sense ‘may give’ or simple present ‘gives’ in a conditional context), and کردگار is the agent ‘the Creator/God.’ This is a conventional petitionary clause.
segments.s351-g40: که introduces a purpose/result clause ‘so that / that,’ and بینم is first-person singular subjunctive (‘I may see’ / ‘I might see’). یکی روی = ‘one face’ (یکی as numeral/indefinite) and آن شهریار ‘that king/ruler’ is the person whose face the speaker longs to see; word order is typical poetic (object phrase after the verb).
segments.s351-g233: چنین گفت is a fixed phrase meaning "he said thus/so" (چنین = thus + گفت = said). با introduces the person addressed (here with the object-marker-like sense 'to'); هرک is an archaic form of هرکس (whoever/anyone), and بد یادگیر is a compound meaning "bad/poor learner" (بد modifies یادگیر). The whole segment = "he said thus to every poor learner."
segments.s351-g234: که introduces reported speech or a content clause ('that' / 'namely'). بیدار باشید is a hortative/imperative plural (باشید) meaning "be awake/alert." برنا و پیر are a paired nominal phrase "young and old," enumerating those to whom the command applies. Together: "that you be awake, young and old."
segments.s351-g235: فرخ‌زاد را uses را to mark the person whose commands are meant (classical object/ topical marking). جمله (= entirely/all) qualifies فرمان (command(s)). فرمان برید functions as an imperative/hortative construction meaning "obey/attend to the command(s)." The segment therefore instructs listeners to follow Farrokhzad's commands completely.
segments.s351-g236: ز is the poetic form of از meaning "from/of/regarding." گفتار و کردار او = "his words and his deeds." مگذرید is a negative imperative/hortative meaning "do not let (them) pass" or "do not overlook/forgive." The clause therefore commands: "do not overlook his speech and action."
segments.s351-g237: ازان (from that) begins the clause; آگهی شد is a passive or resultive expression meaning "news was made/became known" (شد used as an auxiliary/past). به هر کشوری means "to/through every country/realm." The segment says that word of this reached every land.
segments.s351-g238: به هر پادشاهی و هر مهتری continues the distribution: به هر پادشاهی = "to every kingdom/king," and هر مهتری here means "every chief/elder/noble." The repetition of به هر emphasizes that the news reached rulers and leading men everywhere.
segments.s351-g193: ز + noun phrase: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' (here marking source or cause) followed by هیشوی قیصر; word order is preposition + modifier + head. بپرسد is a present/stative-form verb (third person) meaning 'asks/asks about' and سخن is the object 'speech/word' placed after the verb in this compressed, poetic word order.
segments.s351-g194: This clause is a short predication: نوست 'is new' (adjective with present copular sense) followed by این 'this/the present' as its topic; نگشتست is a compound negative-ish verbal form meaning 'did not become/was not turned' (perfective in sense, here stating it never became so); باری کهن is an adverbial phrase 'once/formerly old' (باری = once/formerly, کهن = old/ancient) modifying نگشتست. The sequence gives: 'This is new; it has not once become old.'
segments.s351-g195: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'; هیشوی is used here as the lexical item introduced earlier ('never' in the poem) and شد is the past/preterite 'became/came to be'. پیش + دندان forms a locative phrase 'before/in front of the teeth' (پیش = before/in the presence of, دندان = 'teeth'); ببرد is a third-person singular present/subjunctive form of برد 'to take' used here as a narrative perfective sense 'he took/seized'. The clause means 'when X came before the teeth, he took (it)'.
segments.s351-g196: گذشته سخنها is a noun phrase 'the past/tales' (گذشته = past, سخنها = speeches/tales). برو here functions as a narrative-prefixed verb 'he went on/then' (imperative-like root used narratively) and بر + شمرد is the verbal complex 'to count over' or 'to enumerate' (بر = upon/toward, شمرد = 'counted/enumerated'). Together: 'he then set about counting/enumerating the past tales.'
segments.s351-g197: به + پوزش بیاراست is a prepositional phrase meaning 'with/apologetically he adorned' (به here 'with/with regard to', پوزش = apology/excuse, بیاراست = 'adorned/composed' — a causative/perfective formation). قیصر is the subject 'the Caesar' and زبان (here 'speech/utterance') names what he adorned; literally 'the Caesar set his speech in apology.'
segments.s351-g198: بدو is the dative 'to him/unto him' (marking the addressee), گفت is the verb 'said/spoke' used here in narrative past, followed by the quoted short sentence بیداد رفت 'bēdād went/has happened' (بیداد = injustice/woe, رفت = went/has gone, used idiomatically to mean 'woe/injustice has come to pass'). ای جوان is a direct vocative 'O young man.' The clause thus reports his speech to the youth.
segments.s351-g199: کنون 'now' introduces the question; آن گرامی کتایون is the definite noun phrase 'that honored Katayun' (آن = that/the, گرامی = honored/noble, کتایون = the proper name Katayun). کجاست is the fused interrogative copula 'where is (she)?' — question word کجا + ست (is).
segments.s351-g200: مرا‌گر is a conditional concessive phrase 'if to me / were it to me' (introducing the speaker's hypothetical). ستمگاره is the noun 'oppressor' (here the predicate/content of the putative speech), خواند is the verb 'to call/name' (here conditional: 'if I were to call [someone] an oppressor'), and رواست is the impersonal/final copula 'it is fitting/permissible'. The whole gives a conditional assessment: 'If I called him oppressor, it would be fitting.'
segments.s351-g145: ولیکن is a conjunction meaning 'but/yet' at the start of the clause. ترا‌گر is a conditional phrase (ترا + گر) meaning 'if to you / if you' (an archaic fused form), followed by چنین است رای literally 'such is (the) counsel/opinion' where رای is the predicate ('is such a counsel'). The word order is typical of classical Persian: conditional marker + comment about the addressee's opinion.
segments.s351-g146: نپیچم is negative + present/subjunctive form (ن + پیچم) meaning 'I will not/shall not turn away' (here a first‑person negative stating refusal or non‑deviation). ز رای تو links with نپیچم and means 'from your counsel' (ز = 'from', رای = 'counsel/opinion', تو = 'your'), and ای رهنمای is a vocative 'O guide' addressing the addressee.
segments.s351-g147: This is two past actions placed in sequence: بیامد 'he came/entered' + به میدان 'into the field/arena'. قیصر رسید follows as a second past verb phrase meaning 'he reached/approached the Caesar' (رسید = 'arrived/reached'); juxtaposing two past verbs like this is common in epic narrative.
segments.s351-g148: همی بود تا is a conjunctive progressive formula meaning 'he was just about to / was on the point of' (همی ... بود + تا introduces the moment); تا here functions like 'when/that' leading into the subordinate clause. زخم چوگان is a genitive phrase 'the wound of/from the polo‑mallet' (چوگان = the mallet), and بدید is the past 'he saw'.
segments.s351-g149: ازیشان یکی literally 'one out of them' (از + ایشان = 'of them / from them'), followed by گوی و چوگان بخواست, where گوی ('sphere') = 'the ball' and چوگان = 'the polo‑mallet'; بخواست is the past 'he requested/asked for' (or 'desired'), so the whole clause means 'one of them asked for the ball and the mallet.'
segments.s351-g150: میان سواران is a locative phrase 'into/among the horsemen' (میان = 'among/middle of', سواران = 'riders/horsemen'). برافگند is a past verb 'he threw/cast' and راست functions adverbially 'straight/directly', so the clause says he hurled it straight among the riders.
segments.s351-g151: برانگیخت is a past transitive verb 'he raised/roused' and takes آن بارگی را as its definite object ('that بارگی' with را). ز جای is the source phrase 'from its place' (ز = 'from', جای = 'place'). The clause therefore means 'he raised that بارگی from its place.'
segments.s351-g152: یلان را هم (topic marked by را) introduces 'the heroes' as the affected group; کند شد is a two‑word predicate meaning 'became slow/ineffective' (کند = 'slow', شد = 'became'), and دست و پای 'hands and feet' names the limbs affected. The line states that all the heroes' hands and feet were rendered sluggish/powerless.
segments.s351-g65: بیاورد is a 3rd-person singular past/preterite-form meaning “he brought” (classical Persian often uses this form where modern Persian would use آورد). اهرن here appears as a proper name or unusual term and functions as the direct object (the recipient). بسی is an adverb meaning “much/many” modifying خواسته; خواسته is a nominalized past participle meaning “desired things / requests” and is the object that was brought.
segments.s351-g66: گرانمایه is an adjective ‘precious/valuable’ placed before the noun. اسپان is the plural noun ‘horses’ (or cavalry) and is the head noun; آراسته is a past participle/adjective ‘adorned/arrayed’ that qualifies اسپان (i.e. the horses are adorned). Together they form a coordinated phrase describing the decorated, valuable horses.
segments.s351-g67: یکی is an indefinite subject ‘one / someone’. تیغ برداشت is a SOV clause where تیغ (sword) is the object and برداشت is the past verb ‘took up / drew’. The conjunction و links this clause to the next. یک‌باره (adverb) + جنگ is elliptical: یک‌باره modifies جنگ (‘sudden/instant combat’) with the verb omitted by poetic ellipsis (i.e. ‘there was / began’).
segments.s351-g68: کمانی is an adjective or noun-form meaning ‘of the bow / arched’ referring to archery or bow-weapons. The conjunction و links it to the following nominal phrase. سه چوبه تیر literally ‘three shafts/staves of arrow(s)’ (چوبه = shaft/beam) counts arrows; خدنگ modifies تیر as ‘dagger-like’ (i.e. with dagger-points). Word order groups these weapon terms as the instruments of the sudden fight.
segments.s351-g69: به is the preposition ‘to/for’. هیشوی is a negative adverb ‘never / not at all’ that here negates the giving. داد is the past verb ‘gave’. آن دگر is ‘that other (one)’ (the indirect object/target) and هرچ بود ‘whatever there was’ is a relative/indefinite phrase — بود is the past copula ‘was’. The clause thus states that he never gave that other any of whatever existed.
segments.s351-g70: ز is the poetic preposition ‘of / from’ marking the material or source (here: ‘of dinars’). دینار is the coin/money; the token وز here should be read as the sequence و ز (and of) joining the compounds, so it functions like ‘and of’. جامه is ‘garment(s)’ and نابسود is an adjective ‘fruitless / to no avail’, predicated of the listed goods. The phrase says that whatever there was in money or clothes proved useless.
segments.s351-g71: چنین is an adverb ‘thus / so’ introducing reported speech. گفت is the past verb ‘said’ (poetic word order puts the verb near the front). گشتاسپ is the speaker’s name (Gushtasp). با سرکشان literally ‘with the chiefs’ functions as ‘to/among the nobles’ — با + plural of a word for powerful men; in context it addresses his leading men.
segments.s351-g72: کزین is an archaic compound ‘from this / from him’. کس is ‘anyone / person’; نباید is an impersonal modal ‘should not / must not’. که introduces the clause containing دارد نشان: دارد is the present/past-auxiliary here meaning ‘has / possess(es)’ and نشان is ‘sign / token’. The whole clause forbids anyone from receiving/accepting a token from this man.
segments.s351-g185: نرفت is the past negative of رفت (he did not go). The clause has an inverted order: نرفت (verb) precedes the locational/limiting phrase اندرین جز به آیین شهر (lit. 'into these except by the city's rule'), where اندرین 'in these' + جز 'except' limits the verb and به آیین شهر gives the manner (به ... آیین = 'in the manner/custom of'). This is a compact, poetic way to say he did not enter except according to the city's custom.
segments.s351-g186: ازان ('from that/thereon') picks up the preceding assertion and introduces a result or consequence. راستی here functions as 'uprightness/rightness' rather than a simple abstract 'truth'. خواری is the noun 'humiliation/abase­ment'. آمدش is آمد + enclitic -ش 'to him' (past 'came to him'). بهر expresses purpose/result ('for/on account of'). The sequence means that as a result of that uprightness he suffered humiliation.
segments.s351-g187: به here introduces a locative/scene ('to' or 'in' the place). بیشه درون آن is a nested locative: بیشه 'grove/thicket' qualified by درون آن 'its inner part/within that'. زیانکار is an adjectival epithet ('harm‑doing' / 'harmful') placed before گرگ 'wolf' as a poetic epithet, so the phrase names 'a harmful wolf in that inner grove.'
segments.s351-g188: The second nominal scene is parallel: به کوه بزرگ gives the location ('on/to the great mountain'), followed by the noun phrase اژدهای سترگ. اژدهای is 'dragon/monster' and سترگ is an epithet modifying it; in poetry adjectives or epithets can follow the noun or be stacked for effect. The line therefore contrasts two threatening creatures in two places.
segments.s351-g189: سرانشان 'their chiefs/leaders' is the subject. به زخم من is a causal/instrumental phrase ('by my wound' i.e. wounded by me or struck by my blow). آمد is the past 'came' and the following به پای 'to (my) feet' expresses submission or falling at someone's feet; the verb + به پای idiomatically means they came and submitted at his feet. Word order places the agent/result phrase before the verb for emphasis.
segments.s351-g190: بران here means 'on/over that' or 'concerning that' (it points back to the preceding event). کار is 'affair/deed', so بران کار = 'as for that affair'. هیشوی is an intensifying/time word (here best read as 'at no time / never' modifying the following adjective), بد رهنمای = 'a bad guide/adviser'. Together the phrase condemns the guiding/leadership behind the deed: it was the work of a guide who was never good.
segments.s351-g191: که introduces an explanatory clause 'that/since'. دندانهاشان 'their teeth' is literal but here may stand metonymically for their bite/hostility. بخان is an irregular/contracted poetic form (here to be read as به خان 'in the house/inner chamber'); such contractions and fused prepositional forms occur in Ferdowsi's diction. منست is an archaic copular/predicative form meaning 'is mine / belongs to me' or 'is upon me' in context; poetic morphology often preserves -ست endings attached to pronouns.
segments.s351-g192: همان 'that very / the same' points back to the wound; زخم 'wound' is the noun, خنجر 'dagger' names the weapon, نشان 'sign/mark' is the predicate; منست again is the archaic copular/predicative form. The phrase says the wound is the (visible) mark left by his dagger. Word order (subject — epithet — predicate) is compact and typical of epic verse.
segments.s351-g153: 'به میدان' is a locative/locative-directional prepositional phrase ('to/into the field' or 'on the field') placed at the front for setting; 'کسی' is an indefinite that with the negative verb will mean 'no one'; 'نیز' here functions as 'also/even' emphasizing the negation; 'گویی' is a parenthetical modal ('it seemed' / 'as if') that scopes over the clause; 'ندید' is the simple past negative 'did not see' and lacks an explicit object here (the object 'him' is picked up in the next segment as 'او').
segments.s351-g154: 'شد' is the past-tense copular/auxiliary 'became/was' used with the following predicate; 'از زخم او' is a causal/ablative phrase ('because of/from his wound'), with 'او' the pronoun 'him'; 'در جهان' gives the sphere/location for the predicate ('in/regarding the world' — here idiomatically 'from the world'); 'ناپدید' is the predicate adjective 'vanished/gone'. Together this yields 'he became vanished from the world because of his wound.'
segments.s351-g155: This is a question/word-order inversion: 'سواران' is the subject 'the riders/horsemen', 'کجا' ('where') is a fronted interrogative adverb modifying 'یافتند' (found), and 'گوی او' is the direct object 'his ball/orb' (possession expressed by postnominal pronoun). The verb 'یافتند' is past plural 'they found' — the clause asks rhetorically where the riders could find that ball/thing.
segments.s351-g156: 'به چوگان زدن' is a verbal-nominal phrase meaning 'to strike with a polo-mallet' where 'به' marks instrument/means, 'چوگان' names the mallet, and 'زدن' is the verbal noun/infinitive 'to hit/strike'; 'نیز' functions as 'even/also' intensifying the negation; 'نشتافتند' is past negative of 'شتافتن' ('they did not hasten/they did not manage to hurry'), so the whole clause says they did not even hurry to strike (it) with their mallets.
segments.s351-g157: 'شدند' is the past plural copula 'they became'; 'آن زمان' is a temporal adverbial 'at that time/then'; 'رومیان' is the subject 'the Romans'; 'زردروی' is a predicate adjective 'sallow-faced/yellowish' placed after the subject. Word order is somewhat topicalized/poetic (verb first), but the meaning is 'At that time the Romans became sallow-faced.'
segments.s351-g158: 'همه پاک' is an intensifying phrase: 'همه' (all/everyone) and 'پاک' here as an adverb 'completely/utterly'; 'با' introduces manner ('with'); 'غلغل' is 'uproar/clamor' and 'گفت و گوی' ('گفت' + 'و' + 'گوی') is the noun cluster meaning 'talk/conversation' — thus 'all completely with clamor and talk' (i.e. they were wholly in an uproar and talking).
segments.s351-g159: 'کمان برگرفتند' is 'they took up/lifted bows' — 'برگرفتند' (they lifted/took up) acting as the verb with 'کمان' as its object; 'و' links the second object-phrase; 'تیر خدنگ' is a noun phrase where 'تیر' (arrow) is modified by postpositive adjective 'خدنگ' (dagger-like), so together 'تیر خدنگ' = 'a dagger-pointed arrow' (or short/stout arrow). The clause lists weapons they seized.
segments.s351-g160: 'برفتند' here means 'they went off/advanced' (intransitive motion), followed by the temporal adverb 'چندی' ('for a while'); 'سواران جنگ' is a noun phrase 'the horsemen of war' (or 'the war-horsemen'), with 'جنگ' qualifying 'سواران'. The overall order is verb — adverbial — subject phrase, a common poetic inversion: 'they advanced, for a while, the horsemen of war.'
segments.s351-g73: نه at the start negates the whole clause ('not / it is not that'). از من literally 'from me' functions idiomatically to mean 'by me' or 'as for me' (i.e. 'I have not...'). که introduces the subordinate content clause 'that I have seen...'. نر اژدها is a noun phrase ('male dragon'), with the attributive نر preceding the noun (an original Indo‑Iranian ordering sometimes preserved in poetry). دیده‌ام is a perfective first‑person form 'I have seen' (دیده + ـام), completing the subordinate clause.
segments.s351-g74: گر here is concessive ('though / even if'), introducing the contrasted clause. آواز آن گرگ is the noun phrase 'the voice of that wolf' (آن = 'that' pointing back to a previously mentioned wolf). بشنیده‌ام is the perfective 'I have heard' (بـ‑شـنیده + ـام) and links the clause to the speaker; together the clause means 'even if I have heard the voice of that wolf.'
segments.s351-g75: وزان = 'and/from that' (links to the preceding action or place: 'from that place'). جایگه = 'place' (the locative center). شاد و خرم are two coordinated adjectives 'happy and joyful' describing the subject. برفت is a past tense verb 'he/it went/departed' (third person past); it governs the whole clause, so the phrase reads 'from that place, happy and joyful, he went.'
segments.s351-g76: به سوی introduces a direction 'toward(s)'. کتایون is the proper name 'Katayun' as the goal. خرامید is a past tense verb meaning 'he strode/pranced proudly' and describes his manner of approach. تفت at the end is a short poetic/exclamatory or onomatopoetic particle here (not the common lexical senses 'roast/char'); it functions as a metrical/expressive appendage and does not change the verb’s syntax.
segments.s351-g77: بشد is a narrative past/prefatory 'it happened / it came to pass'. اهرن is an uncertain term or proper name here (likely a name or archaic word) and functions as the subject or theme. و is the simple coordinator 'and'. گاو گردون is a compound-like phrase 'the cow/ox of the sky' or 'heavenly ox' (گاو 'cow/ox' modified by گردون 'sky/heaven'). ببرد is a 3rd‑person verb form (بـ‑برد), here meaning 'took/carried' (the b‑prefix marks the present/subjunctive stem in New Persian but in narrative can express ongoing/resultive action).
segments.s351-g78: تن اژدها is a genitival noun phrase 'the body of the dragon' (تن = 'body' followed by the possessor اژدها). کهتران here is a recipient name/group (not the conjunction که) — the form functions as the indirect object/recipient. را is the definite object/topic marker attached for emphasis/definiteness, and سپرد is the simple past 'he entrusted/handed over' (transitive), so the clause means 'he handed over the dragon’s body to the Kheteran(s).'
segments.s351-g79: که introduces a relative clause ('which/that') referring back to the object just mentioned. این را = 'this (thing) (را)' — the demonstrative plus the definite object marker. به درگاه قیصر is the prepositional phrase 'to the court/courtly presence of the Caesar' (به = 'to', درگاه = 'royal court/presence', قیصر = 'Caesar, the emperor'). برید is a past transitive verb 'they took/carried' (here 'they brought it to the court').
segments.s351-g80: به پیش = 'before/in the presence of' (به + پیش). بزرگان لشگر is a genitival/attributive phrase 'the great ones/chiefs of the army' (بزرگان = 'great ones/elders', لشگر = 'army'). برید repeats the past transitive verb 'they brought/carried (it)' and here takes the previously mentioned این را as its object (understood from the prior segment): 'they brought it before the army’s chiefs.'
segments.s351-g57: This is a VSO/OV flexible order typical of Persian poetry: دیده‌ام is the perfect (have-seen) 1st person singular verb (‘I have seen’). چنین اژدها is a noun phrase ‘such a dragon’ (چنین = ‘such’ modifying اژدها = ‘dragon’). من placed after the noun is emphatic (‘I’), and بسی before the verb intensifies the verb (‘many/often’).
segments.s351-g58: که introduces a relative clause modifying اژدها (‘that/whose’). از marks an ablative/agentive relation here (‘in/against’ the battle of/with him). او is the possessor/agent ‘him/his’. سر نپیچیده‌ام is a negative perfect: ن- prefix + پیچیده (past participle of پیچیدن ‘to turn’), plus ـ‌ام ‘I have’ = ‘I have not turned my head’; idiomatically ‘I did not turn aside/retreat’ in battle. The clause therefore qualifies the dragons just mentioned.
segments.s351-g59: شنیدند is the 3rd-person plural past ‘they heard’. The names (or nouns) هیشوی and اهرن follow the verb as the subjects — a common poetic inversion. سخن is the direct object ‘speech/words’. (If هیشوی/اهرن are proper names they function here as the listeners.)
segments.s351-g60: ازان نو is a temporal/linear phrase ‘from that, anew’ (ازان = ‘from that’, نو = ‘new/again’). به governs the manner or direction here ‘in/with’ and introduces گفتار ‘speech/style’. دانش کهن is a genitive phrase ‘ancient knowledge’ modifying گفتار (‘speech in the style of ancient learning’). The whole segment says they resumed speaking in the old learned style.
segments.s351-g61: چو is the temporal conjunction ‘when’. آواز او is the noun phrase ‘his voice’. آن دو گردن‌فراز is a descriptive noun phrase ‘those two, proud/lofty ones’ (آن دو = ‘those two’; گردن‌فراز = adjective/noun ‘proud’). The phrase sets the temporal scene for the verbs that follow.
segments.s351-g62: شنیدند و بردند are two coordinated 3rd-person plural past verbs ‘they heard and they took/carried/presented’. پیشش (پیش + ـش) means ‘before him / in his presence’ (پیش + 3rd-singular enclitic). نماز is the object here: literal ‘prayer’ but in epic-poetic usage can mean an act of salute or ritual obeisance. Together: ‘they listened and presented prayer before him.’
segments.s351-g63: به گشتاسپ گفتند is a conventional reporting clause: به + proper name marks the addressee ‘to Gushtasp’, and گفتند is 3rd-person plural past ‘they said’. The following phrase کی نره شیر is the content quoted/asked: کی = ‘who/which’, نره شیر = ‘male lion’ (a metaphor for a manly/brave man). The sequence shows reported interrogative speech introduced directly after گفتند.
segments.s351-g64: که introduces the relative/interrogative clause ‘who…’. چون تو = ‘like you’ (چون = ‘as/like’, تو = ‘you’). نزاید is the classical-present/optative form of زاییدن ‘to beget/give birth’ used here in the sense ‘is born/comes into being’ (3rd-singular); in context it says ‘who, like you, is born of a brave mother’. ز marks origin ‘from’, مادر = ‘mother’, دلیر = ‘brave’. The clause qualifies نره شیر in the question.
segments.s351-g41: This is a noun phrase introduced by دگر (an index-word meaning 'another/other') followed by a personal epithet/name. چهر فرخ functions as a compound name/epithet ('fair-/bright-faced Farrokh') and برادر زریر is an appositive phrase identifying him as 'brother of Zarihr'. Word order is determiner (دگر) → name/epithet → relational apposition.
segments.s351-g42: بگویم is a present-subjunctive/jussive form used narratively as 'I will say' or 'let me tell'; که introduces the content clause. گشتم is a past perfective of گشتن here meaning 'I became' or 'I grew (to be)', with the subject من following the verb (inversion common in poetic Persian). از + noun + adjective (از تاج سیر) expresses the state 'sated/weary of the crown' (سیر as predicate meaning 'satiated/weary of').
segments.s351-g43: Again بگویم 'I will say/let me tell' introduces the clause. The clause بر من چه آمد ز بخت is an idiomatic expression: بر من آمد = 'befell me' and چه (interrogative) asks 'what'; ز بخت ('from fate') gives the cause/agent. Word order places the prepositional phrase بر من before the verb آمد.
segments.s351-g44: همی is an adverb meaning 'continually/still' modifying the verb phrase. تخت جستم is a past-tense clause with جستم (from جستن) meaning 'I sought' and تخت 'the throne' as its object. که introduces the following clause 'that the throne was lost' (an explanatory/sequence כe): گم گشت تخت = 'the throne became lost' (گشت as copular/become + گم 'lost'), with the noun تخت repeated as the affected thing.
segments.s351-g45: پر از آب رخ بارگی is a compact descriptive phrase: پر (an adjective-like word 'full') is followed by the preposition از + noun (آب) to yield 'full of water'. رخ is the noun 'face' and بارگی is a poetic/archaic modifier attached to رخ (here indicating the condition or quality of the face—moist/tearful). برنشست is the simple past (3rd-person) of برنشتن/نشستن with the prefix بر- meaning 'sat up' or 'rose onto (a seat)'; word order here is descriptive phrase → verb, so: '(with) face full of water, he sat up.'
segments.s351-g46: همان خنجر آب‌داده به دست is a noun phrase identifying the weapon. همان is a demonstrative 'that/the same' pointing back to an earlier-mentioned dagger; خنجر is the noun 'dagger'. آب‌داده is a compound adjective 'water-given' i.e. 'wet/drenched' (poetic formation meaning 'having been given water'). به دست is a simple locative/possessive phrase 'to/into the hand' used idiomatically for 'in (his) hand' — the whole gives 'that same dagger, wet, in his hand.'
segments.s351-g47: چو here is the temporal subordinating conjunction 'when'. نزدیک is an adverb/adjective 'near' (here 'drew near'). هیشوی and اهرن are treated as proper names (two parties/persons) joined by و 'and'. رسید is the simple past 'arrived/reached'. The clause therefore reads 'when X and Y came near/arrived'.
segments.s351-g48: همه یاد کرد is an idiomatic past construction: همه 'everyone' is subject, یاد کردن as a compound verb = 'to remember' (here used as یاد + کرد, literally 'made remembrance'). آن شگفتی 'that wonder/marvel' is the object, and the relative clause که دید 'that they had seen' specifies which wonder. Note that یاد کردن uses a noun + doing-verb pattern rather than a single lexical verb.
segments.s351-g1: بشد is an archaic/past intransitive meaning “it became / it happened” and here introduces the coming-into-being or arrival of the following noun. اهرن is a proper name (or uncommon term) serving as the predicate of بشد. و links the first clause to the following one; هرچ is an indefinite 'whatever', and گشتاسپ خواست is a past-tense clause 'Gushtasp wanted' where خواست is the simple past 'wanted'.
segments.s351-g2: بیاورد is the verbal complement meaning 'to bring' and here completes خواست (i.e. 'wanted to bring'); the construction خواست بیاورد = 'wished to bring'. چون begins a temporal clause 'when/whenas' (classical use overlaps with 'as/when'), کارها is the plural noun 'affairs/matters' and گشت (from گشتن) + راست 'turned (right)' = 'the affairs turned/ became right' (گشت as 'turn/become', راست as 'right/true').
segments.s351-g3: ز دریا is a poetic source phrase 'from the sea'. به زین اندر is the directional phrase 'into the saddle' (به + زین + اندر = 'into the saddle'), and آورد is the past verb 'brought/put'. پای is the direct object 'foot' — literal order: 'from the sea into the saddle he put (his) foot.'
segments.s351-g4: برفتند is past 3rd plural 'they went/left/rose'. یارانش is the subject 'his companions' with the possessive suffix written separately here as ش. با او 'with him' marks accompaniment, and ز جای 'from the place' gives the point of departure. Note the verb precedes its subject here, a poetic word order.
segments.s351-g5: چو = 'when/as'. بدید is the simple past 'saw'. کوه + سقیلا is a noun phrase 'the mountain Saqilā' (sقیلا appears as a rare/obscure epithet or name); هیشوی is an archaic/poetic element whose lexical senses listed do not fit well here and functions as an epithet or modifier of the mountain. The whole segment is a temporal clause: 'when he saw [that mountain]'.
segments.s351-g6: به انگشت is an instrumental/locative phrase 'with/by a finger' (به used here to mark instrument). بنمود is the past of نمودن 'show/point/indicate' — 'he pointed'. و links the clauses. خود را is the emphatic/accusative 'himself' (خود plus object marker را), and کشید is a past verb 'pulled/drew' (here reflexive: 'drew/pulled himself' or 'drew himself up'); کشید is an older form and the exact nuance depends on context (pull/draw/jerk).
segments.s351-g7: خود و اهرن is a coordinated subject 'he himself and Ahran'. از جای is the source phrase 'from the place/spot'. گشتند is past 3rd plural of گشتن used here as 'they turned/moved/left' and باز is the adverb 'again', so the clause reads 'he and Ahran again moved from the place.'
segments.s351-g8: چو = 'as/when' introducing the simile. خورشید is the noun 'the sun' (subject of the comparison). برزد is an archaic poetic verb (here conveying a forceful action such as 'struck/shot/shot forth') whose provided modern senses do not exactly match the classical use, so it is best treated as an archaism meaning something like 'casts/strikes forth'. سنان is a plural/collective noun 'spears' (used metaphorically for rays or lances), and از فراز = 'from on high' (فراز = height). The line thus compares their departure to the sun casting spears from above.
segments.s351-g217: نگوید همی is a classical/poetic present construction: همی is the continuous particle and نگوید is the negative 3rd-person present form (ن‑ + گوید), so together it means “he does not say / he does not tell.” پیش من is a prepositional phrase “before/in the presence of me,” and راز خویش is a possessive noun phrase (“his secret”), where خویش is split into خوی + ش (possessive enclitic).
segments.s351-g218: نهان دارد is a light adjectival/adverbial + verb combination: نهان (hidden/secret) modifies دارد (present 3rd-person ‘has/keeps’), so the clause means ‘he keeps (it) hidden.’ از هرکس introduces the source/target (‘from/everyone’), and آواز خویش is the object (‘his voice/utterance’), again with خوی + ش forming the possessive.
segments.s351-g219: گمانم is a first-person comment (“I suppose / I imagine”), with the enclitic ـم marking the speaker. که introduces a subordinate clause (‘that’), هست is the copula ‘is,’ and از نژاد بزرگ is a genitive phrase ‘of/from a great lineage’ (از + noun for ‘of’ in older/poetic Persian).
segments.s351-g220: که here resumes the relative sense ‘who/which.’ پرخاش جویست is an adjectival relative: پرخاش (reproach/attack) + جویست (archaic 3rd-person form of ‘seek’ = ‘is one who seeks’), so ‘one who seeks reproach’ → ‘quarrelsome’ or ‘given to reproach.’ و connects phrases; گرد and سترگ are coordinated epithets describing appearance — گرد used adjectivally (literally ‘round/around’) and سترگ relates to sight/eye, so together they paint a picture like ‘round and large-eyed.’
segments.s351-g221: ز is the poetic preposition ‘from’ or ‘when/after’ used to introduce the circumstance. هرچش (an archaic/poetic form) means ‘in any way/whatever’ or ‘from any quarter,’ and بپرسم is a 1st‑person present/subjunctive form (ب‑ prefix + پرسـ stem) ‘I ask’ (roughly ‘if/when I ask’). نگوید is the negative 3rd‑person present ‘he does not say,’ and تمام here functions adverbially/nominally as ‘completely / the whole of it.’
segments.s351-g222: فرخ‌زاد is a proper name (here the speaker). گوید is the 3rd‑person present of گفتن ‘says’ (classical present form). که introduces reported content (‘that’); هستم is 1st‑person present ‘I am’; به نام is a fixed prepositional phrase meaning ‘by name’ or ‘called (by the name).’ The clause reports Farrokhzad’s words: ‘Farrokhzad says that I am (so) by name.’
segments.s351-g223: وزان is a fused poetic prepositional phrase meaning ‘and from that / thence,’ جایگه is a poetic word for ‘place/spot,’ سوی + noun is the usual ‘toward/onto’ construction (سوی ایوان = ‘toward the iwan/porch’), and گذشت is the simple past/past‑narrative verb ‘he passed/went’ (classical past tense).
segments.s351-g224: سپهر is a poetic term for ‘sky/heaven’; اندرین = اندر + این (poetic) ‘in/therein’; نیز = ‘also’; چندی = ‘for a while’; and بگشت here is an archaic/periphrastic past or narrative verb (contextually ‘turned / passed / remained for a while’) — the clause describes that, in that place/sky, time (or the heavens) likewise moved/turned for a short time.
segments.s351-g169: سرافراز گردان بسی دیده‌ام is a compact clause: سرافراز (an epithet) modifies گردان (troop), بسی is an adverbial intensifier 'many/much' (classical usage), and دیده‌ام is the perfect first‑person singular 'I have seen'. Word order is adjective + noun + adverb + verb, so literally 'I have seen many a lofty troop.'
segments.s351-g170: بدین گونه is the prepositional phrase 'in/of this manner' (ب = به 'to/at', دین here representing این in the orthography), گونه = 'manner, way'; نشنیده‌ام is the negative perfect 'I have not heard of / I have not heard' (used here to mean 'I have not encountered/seen/ heard of'). The literal order is 'rider in this manner I have not heard,' i.e. 'I have not heard of a rider like this.'
segments.s351-g171: بخوانید is an imperative/plural form here meaning 'summon/call' (classical Persian uses خواندن also for calling); تا introduces purpose 'so that / in order that'; ز (here 'از') + او (written as و in the segmentation) = 'from him / of him' and governs the embedded verb بپرسم 'I may ask' (بپرسم is subjunctive/optative: 'that I ask'). که introduces the content clause 'who he is' (کیست: کی 'who' + ست copula 'is').
