segments.s351-g172: This is the content of the question introduced by که: فرشتست‌گر asks about the rider's nature ('an angel' or divine being) and همچو ما آدمیست gives the contrast 'or like us, is he a man'. آدمیست is the predicate with the copula suffix -ست 'is'; the coordination gives an either/or sense.
segments.s351-g173: بخواندند is a past plural 'they summoned/called'; گشتاسپ را uses را as the definite/direct‑object marker; پیش اوی 'before him / into his presence' places Gشتاسپ in front of the addressee. The verb precedes object+prepositional phrase which is normal in this poetic register.
segments.s351-g174: بپیچید is a past/formal verb here 'twisted/turned' (poetic past); جان بداندیش اوی is a nominal phrase 'his ill‑intentioned soul' (جان = 'soul, life', بداندیش = 'ill‑minded, malicious', اوی = 'his/he'). The verb+noun order and the adjective‑noun coupling convey that his malicious heart 'turned' or was stirred — a poetic way to say he became hostile or plotted.
segments.s351-g175: به گشتاسپ گفت is 'he said to Gushtasp' (به = to/for, گفت here functions as 'said' though the listed sense set is imperfect); ای is the vocative 'O!' and نبرده سوار is a vocative phrase 'un‑taken/unconquered rider' (نبرده functioning adjectivally). Word order is 'to Gushtasp he said: "O ..."'.
segments.s351-g176: This is a string of honorific appositions: سر سرکشان = 'head/chief of the chiefs' (سر = 'head/leader', سرکشان here plural/genitive), and افسر کارزار = 'helmet (or crown) of the battle' (افسر in Old Persian poetic usage = helmet, کارزار = battlefield/battle). They are epithets addressing the rider, piled after the vocative.
segments.s351-g9: This is a simple nominal clause: جهانجوی (the subject, 'the world-seeker' or ambitious man) is followed by a locative prepositional phrase بر پیش آن کوه ('before that mountain') and the past copula بود ('was/stood'). In classical/poetic Persian the order Subject + locative + بود is normal; بود is 3rd-person singular past. 'بر پیش' is a compound locative meaning 'in front of / before'.
segments.s351-g10: که introduces a relative clause modifying 'that mountain' (i.e. 'where/which'), followed by آرام (adjective/adverb 'calm') + آن مار ('that serpent') and the past copula بود ('was'). The sequence آرام آن مار نستوه بود literally says 'calm that serpent (was) نستوه' — poetically describing the serpent's state; بود again is past tense 3rd singular.
segments.s351-g11: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'; آن اژدهابرز is the topical/subject noun phrase ('that Ozhdahābarz' — a dragon-name/epithet). او را is the object 'him' with the definite object marker را, and بدید is the past-tense verb 'saw' (3rd singular). Word order places the temporal particle first, then subject, object, and verb.
segments.s351-g12: به دم ('with [its] breath' or 'with a breath') is an adverbial phrase; سوی خویشش is a directional phrase 'toward its own [body]' (سوی = 'toward', خویشش = خویش + possessive -ش = 'its own'); همی is a classical particle marking an ongoing/instantaneous action in narrative past, and درکشید is the past verb 'drew in / inhaled' (3rd singular). Together they mean the dragon drew in its breath toward itself.
segments.s351-g13: چو 'when' introduces the subordinate temporal clause. از پیش زین اندر آویخت ترگ is a locative/nominal clause: از پیش ('from the front'), زین ('the saddle'), اندر آویخت (past verb 'hung down / was suspended within/on'), and ترگ ('a rag / shred') as the thing hanging. Note the poetic order where the verb آویخت follows its locative markers.
segments.s351-g14: برو here functions as a brief locative/adverbial element ('thereon' / 'upon that' in classical usage), followed by تیر بارید ('arrows rained' — تیر = 'arrow(s)', بارید = 'rained/fell'). همچون تگرگ is a simile 'like hail' modifying the manner of the falling arrows. The verb بارید is past tense 3rd singular (poetic use of an inanimate subject).
segments.s351-g15: چو 'when' begins the clause. تنگ اندر آمد is a verbal phrase 'came into narrowness / the space grew tight / pressed in' (تنگ = 'narrow', اندر آمد = 'came in/entered' or 'became internal/close'). بران اژدها places the action relative to the dragon ('upon/against that dragon' or 'toward that dragon'); in the narrative this describes the dragon moving into the confined spot. آمد is past tense 3rd singular.
segments.s351-g16: همی جست is a compact verbal phrase: همی marks the vivid/continuous narrative aspect and جست is the past of 'leap/spring' (3rd singular here). مرد جوان is the subject noun phrase 'the young man' (مرد as 'man/hero', جوان qualifying). ز او رها (here written as separate particles) means 'from it, free' — ز = 'from', و = conjunction/particle linking, رها = 'free/let loose' used adjectivally/verbally to indicate he freed himself. Together: 'the young man sprang away, freeing himself from it.'
segments.s351-g209: 'همه دوده را' is the definite/accusative object marked by 'را' (object precedes the verb, as often in classical Persian). 'سر' is the noun 'head/top' that is the thing being raised, and 'برافراختی' is the past, 2nd-person singular verb 'you raised/erected' (ending -ی supplies the subject 'you'). The whole segment literally: 'you raised the head of all the smoke.'
segments.s351-g210: 'برین' here is the fused form of 'بر' + 'این' = 'بر این', meaning 'upon this'. It modifies 'نیکبختی' ('good fortune'). The relative clause 'که تو ساختی' ('which you made') is introduced by 'که' and contains the explicit pronoun 'تو' and the past tense verb 'ساختی' ('you made'), so 'that you made' qualifies 'this good fortune.'
segments.s351-g211: The prepositional phrase 'به پرسش' (= 'to the question' / 'in response to the question') introduces the act of replying. 'بدو گفت' = 'he said to him' (بدو = 'to him'; گفت = past 'said'). 'ز انباز خویش' is a prepositional phrase with 'ز' = 'from' and 'انباز خویش' = 'from his own anbâz' (انباز is an archaic/uncertain noun here and 'خویش' = 'his own').
segments.s351-g212: 'مگر' introduces an exception/conditional sense here ('unless' / 'except that'). 'بر تو' = 'to you' (literally 'upon you' used as indirect object). 'پیدا کند' is a present/subjunctive-style construction with 'کند' (3rd-person) meaning 'may become apparent / be found' (i.e., 'be revealed'). 'راز خویش' = 'his secret' (خویش = his/one's own, here split into خوی + ش). The whole clause: 'unless his secret becomes known to you.'
segments.s351-g213: 'که' introduces the content clause (what might be told). The series 'آرام و شهر و نژادش' lists three items ('abode/home' (آرام), 'city' (شهر), and 'his lineage' (نژاد + possessive ش)). 'کجاست' = 'where is' (کجا + ست 'is'), so together: 'where his abode, city, and lineage are.'
segments.s351-g214: 'بگوید' is a 3rd-person form meaning 'he would say / he may tell' (optative/subjunctive sense). 'مگر' again = 'unless'. The short clause 'مر ترا گفت راست' uses archaic 'مر' = 'me' (object), 'ت' = the 2nd-person enclitic (here written before the object marker 'را'), and 'را' = object marker; 'گفت' = 'said/told' (past), and 'راست' = 'true' / 'truth' (here expressing 'truly' or 'the truth'). Roughly: 'unless, as he told me, (about) you, truly.' Grammatically this is an exception clause introduced by مگر attached to the preceding potential 'بگوید.'
segments.s351-g215: 'چنین داد پاسخ' = 'he gave such an answer / he answered thus' (چنین = 'thus', داد = 'gave', پاسخ = 'answer'). The following 'که پرسیدمش' ('that I asked him') is a subordinate clause: 'پرسیدم' = 'I asked' with the enclitic '-ش' = 'him' (so 'I asked him').
segments.s351-g216: The negation 'نه' (not) applies to the whole clause. 'بر دامن راستی' is a poetic locative phrase ('on the hem/lap of truth' i.e. 'in the guise/position of truthfulness'). 'دیدمش' = 'I saw him' (دیدم + enclitic -ش 'him'). So the sense is 'I did not see him in/at the seat of truthfulness' (= 'I did not find him truthful').
segments.s351-g161: چو introduces a temporal clause meaning 'when'. آن refers back to the preceding event ('that'). دید is a past-tense verb 'saw/beheld'. گشتاسپ is the subject (the king) and برخاست is the following past tense verb 'rose/stood up'. و گفت links the physical action to the speech that follows ('and said'). Word order here places the temporal clause first, then the subject and its actions.
segments.s351-g162: که introduces reported content ('that'). اکنون = 'now'. هنرها (plural) is the object 'feats/skills/deeds'. نشاید is an impersonal/modal present meaning 'it is not fitting/ought not'. نهفت is a past/adjectival form meaning 'to hide / be hidden' and here functions as the verb idea governed by نشاید: 'it is not fitting to hide (them)'. Persian places the modal/impersonal word before the action it governs.
segments.s351-g163: بیفگند is a past-tense verb 'threw/cast (down)'. چوگان is the direct object 'polo mallet' and appears after the verb (typical for the antique/poetic style). کمان برگرفت is a second coordinated action: کمان 'bow' + برگرفت 'took up/raised' (past). The two actions are presented in sequence (he cast the mallet away and then picked up the bow).
segments.s351-g164: This clause lists items taken from someone indicated by ازو ('from him/it') and ends with گرفت (past 'took/seized'). زه = 'bowstring' and توز is an archaic/obscure noun here paired with زه as things taken; ازو marks the source. دست بر سر گرفت is a light-verb construction meaning literally 'took hand on (the) head' — here 'دست بر سر' is the phrase followed by گرفت 'seized/laid', i.e. he laid his hand upon the (head/top). The exact referent of توز is archaic/uncertain in this line, so the gloss remains literal.
segments.s351-g165: نگه کرد is a compound verbal expression 'cast a look / looked' where نگه (gaze/watch) combines with کرد (did) to form the verb. قیصر is the actor (the Caesar) and بران is the fused prepositional phrase 'بر + آن' = 'at/on that' pointing to the person observed. سرفراز is an epithet 'proud/victorious' describing the person being looked at. Word order places the verb phrase first then the observer and the prepositional target.
segments.s351-g166: بدان functions as به + آن ('at that' / 'toward that') and introduces the things he inspected. چنگ, یال and رکیب are a coordinated list of nouns that follow; چنگ (here) is a concrete feature (literally 'claw'/'talon' or a related image), یال = 'mane', and رکیب دراز is a noun plus adjective 'rakīb (companion/rider) long' — the adjective دراز follows the noun it modifies. The sequence names what the Caesar's gaze fell upon.
segments.s351-g167: بپرسید is a past-tense verb 'asked' (3rd person). و گفت links the asking to the utterance that follows. این سوار از کجاست is the embedded question phrase 'this rider is from where?' — از کجا 'from where' and ست is the copula 'is' placed at the end as in classical Persian question structure.
segments.s351-g168: که introduces a relative/subordinate clause 'who/that'. چندین here means 'several/often' or 'repeatedly' (an adverbial sense). بپیچد is the present/subjunctive-form verb 'turns/keeps turning' (the b- prefix + present root is habitual/continuous in context). چپ و دست راست is an idiomatic pairing: چپ = 'to the left', and دست راست = 'to the right' (دست here part of the directional phrase), so the clause describes repeated turning to left and right.

sentences.s351-s65: He said to Gushtasp, “O sullen one sitting there, what thoughts has your heart that make it sorrowful?”
sentences.s351-s66: Two chief nobles went to Rome, who came with crown and with treasure and with helmet.
sentences.s351-s67: One (of them), the one who slew the male dragon, endured many calamities and did not show his back (i.e. did not turn and flee).
sentences.s351-s68: The other, the one who tore the wolf's hide, was throughout all Rome utterly famed.
sentences.s351-s69: In Caesar's arena, amid shame and battle, they raise dust into the sky.
sentences.s351-s70: Look there where Caesar stood — would that not have made your heart's pain any less?
sentences.s351-s71: Gushtasp said to him, 'O fair‑faced one, when did Caesar ever give me justice and favour?'
sentences.s351-s72: He will drive you out of the city with me; when the people see me, what will he do?
sentences.s351-s113: When Gushtasp rose at dawn, the wise‑headed man turned toward the Caesar.
sentences.s351-s114: When the Caesar saw him he kept silent, and he seated that renowned man before his court.
sentences.s351-s115: He demanded a girdle from the treasure and a ring, and one famed diadem of preeminence.
sentences.s351-s116: He kissed him and then placed it upon his head; from past deeds he recalled many (things).
sentences.s351-s9: He placed the light dagger into its mouth and recalled the good gift granted by the Creator.
sentences.s351-s10: He struck that sharp tooth with his dagger; all the blades ended up in its mouth.
sentences.s351-s11: He bathed the hill entirely in poison and blood; poison poured from it until it grew weak.
sentences.s351-s12: At that moment the lion seized the sword in his paw and struck the brave dragon on the head.
sentences.s351-s13: His brain poured out onto that hard stone; from the blow he cried, “O fortunate one.”
sentences.s351-s14: He pulled out from its mouth the two foremost teeth; then he came forward and washed head and body.
sentences.s351-s15: With a roar they tumbled upon the dust before the triumphant Lord.
sentences.s351-s16: Where was that great establishment (power) against that wolf and that mighty dragon?
sentences.s351-s61: His two grooms entered the arena and made his heart glad.
sentences.s351-s62: They wheeled their reins for practice with arrow and with the polo‑mallet and with spear‑wounds.
sentences.s351-s63: They continued charging left and right; as you said, a mounted force suits them.
sentences.s351-s64: Thus, when that exalted day arose, Katayun, the teacher, arrived.
sentences.s351-s49: Thereupon the dragon held a feast for someone; at nightfall the world turned lapis-blue.
sentences.s351-s50: When the sun set a crown upon the sky, the ivory face took on the hue of molten gold.
sentences.s351-s51: Caesar's envoy called for the ceiling to be read, asked, and seated (him) upon the golden throne.
sentences.s351-s52: From the road and from the city's Catholicoi: whoever among the people was (so) —
sentences.s351-s45: From the sky came the lark's cry; it said, “The body of the celestial cow has no radiance.”
sentences.s351-s46: Whoever saw that sword-wound heard the celestial cow roar.
sentences.s351-s47: They kept saying that this dagger was (of) Ahrn (or named Ahrn), and that the wound of Shiravazgan belonged to Ahriman.
sentences.s351-s48: At once the Caesar drove (them) from the iwan and summoned the great men and the sages.
sentences.s351-s101: Mirin and Ahran rose up in anger and said that no word would remain concealed.
sentences.s351-s102: At that moment he dismounted from the swift-footed one and came forward to apologize and declare his pure intent.
sentences.s351-s103: He praised the son greatly: Maran, chaste and wise.
sentences.s351-s104: The Caesar said to him, 'O moon-faced one, you have chosen to bring about your own undoing.'
sentences.s351-s89: Tell me what his name is, and what city and lineage he comes from; to this question he gave him no answer.
sentences.s351-s90: Thus said that lowly foreign man, who had driven him away from the Caesar's city.
sentences.s351-s91: When I became a son‑in‑law and was driven from my city, no one in his bureau read my name aloud.
sentences.s351-s92: Oppression from the Caesar befell Katayun, who chose a strange man from among them.
sentences.s351-s41: He himself went ahead of the herds and the vault of heaven; he strutted up near Caesar — (taft).
sentences.s351-s42: News reached the interior of Rome; the experienced men hurried forward.
sentences.s351-s43: When the ox came down into the plain from the mountain, a terrible roar arose among the host.
sentences.s351-s44: From that wound and that grim dragon there came upon the celestial ox oppression (harm).
sentences.s351-s57: That no one in the world has seen two grooms like mine — none greater than the heavens and the guests.
sentences.s351-s58: They wrote letters to every chief, asking, 'Where did you have a throne-maker and a diadem?'
sentences.s351-s59: So that the male dragon and the proud wolf were ruined by the hands of two mighty men.
sentences.s351-s60: One spectacle he set up before his iwan, raising it like his radiant throne.
sentences.s351-s53: The assembly went into the presence of Skuba; the worldly-wise (members) with the Caesar and the woman's counsel.
sentences.s351-s54: They handed his daughter over to Ahran, and then left her under her mother's kind direction.
sentences.s351-s55: When the people dispersed from the iwan, the famed heart was revived by that speech.
sentences.s351-s56: Thus he said, 'Today is my day; the lofty heaven, heart‑brightening, is mine.'
sentences.s351-s25: He said to Ahrun thus: that the dragon was sharpened by this dagger—priceless.
sentences.s351-s26: You were filled with fear by the great dragon’s breath because of the wolf’s deed.
sentences.s351-s27: Mine is the battlefield of brave, high-minded chiefs, exalted, with heavy maces.
sentences.s351-s28: A very keen strike comes from the whale’s fight, which rises from the deep to battle.
sentences.s351-s17: Lohrasp and Farrokh-Zarir kept saying, "Gushtasp became sated in body and soul."
sentences.s351-s18: With clear soul and heart and strength and radiance — truly they do not see us in sleep.
sentences.s351-s19: I see nothing from the world except suffering and hardship, poison like opium spread over the land.
sentences.s351-s20: Unless the Creator grant (me) life, that I may see the one face of that king.
sentences.s351-s117: He said thus to every poor learner: "Be awake, young and old."
sentences.s351-s118: Give full heed to Farrokhzad's commands; do not pass over his words and deeds.
sentences.s351-s119: From that, news spread to every land, to every kingdom and every chief.
sentences.s351-s98: When 'never' came before the teeth, he seized (it) and began to recount past words.
sentences.s351-s99: The Caesar, with an apologetic tongue, spoke to him: 'Woe/ injustice has come to pass, O young man.'
sentences.s351-s100: Now where is that honored Katayun? If I were to call (someone) an oppressor, it would be fitting.
sentences.s351-s73: But if such is your counsel, I will not turn away from your advice, O guide.
sentences.s351-s74: He entered the field and came up to the Caesar; he was just about to act when he saw the wound from the polo‑mallet.
